name: Marty Tuley
occupation: Author, Personal Trainer, Former Gym Owner
family: Married to Lovena Tuley; one son referenced in earlier book biography
whoTheyAre:
Marty Tuley is a Democratic candidate for governor running as a political outsider from Lawrence. He calls himself a fourth-generation Kansan whose family is made up of farmers, teachers, and working people. He is not a career politician. His professional background is in health and fitness: he spent more than fifteen years as a personal trainer, owned and operated Total Fitness Athletic Center in the Lawrence area, competed as a natural bodybuilder and powerlifter, and published two books on fitness and weight loss. He took out his first SBA loan at age 23 and describes himself as a small-business person first.

Tuley announced his candidacy in July 2025 on a platform of common sense, Kansas values, and bold solutions. His campaign is built almost entirely on social media outreach and his personal website rather than on institutional Democratic Party support. He is married to Lovena Tuley, and his campaign materials emphasize his farming roots and classroom upbringing as the son of two public school teachers who worked on his grandparents' farm.

In a Democratic primary field dominated by Ethan Corson's establishment endorsements and Cindy Holscher's legislative record, Tuley occupies a distinct lane as the only candidate with zero prior elected office. His campaign is the smallest in the field by every measurable standard, but he has participated in the Democratic gubernatorial debate and maintains an active presence on Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, and TikTok.
theirRecord:
Tuley has no record in elected office. He has never held a government position beyond his campaign for governor. That means there are no votes to evaluate, no bills to credit or criticize, and no legislative track record to examine. His candidacy rests entirely on his biography, his campaign platform, and his public statements.

His most distinctive policy positions set him apart from the rest of the Democratic field. He opposes transgender athletes competing in girls' sports, a stance that breaks sharply from mainstream Democratic orthodoxy and is likely the first thing voters will notice about his campaign. He supports legalizing marijuana and directing tax revenue to public schools, raising teacher pay, providing two healthy meals a day for every Kansas public school child, and opposing school vouchers. His health and fitness platform calls for daily physical education in public schools, eliminating padded football for children under thirteen, and promoting farm-to-table meals to fight obesity.

On the business side, Tuley's ownership of Total Fitness Athletic Center in Lawrence involved legal battles reported by the Lawrence Journal-World in 2003. The details of those legal issues are part of the public record but were not fully explored in available research. His two published books and competitive bodybuilding career give him an unusual resume for a gubernatorial candidate, though none of it translates directly into governing experience.
whatYouShouldKnow: Tuley has never held elected office. His candidacy is based entirely on his personal biography, fitness career, and campaign message rather than a political track record.
He is the only Democrat in the governor's race who publicly opposes transgender athletes competing in girls' sports, a position that separates him from Corson and Holscher.
His 2025 year-end campaign finance filing shows just $2,600 raised and $2,406 cash on hand, making his campaign the smallest in the entire governor's field by a wide margin.
He owned and operated Total Fitness Athletic Center in Lawrence, which faced legal battles reported by the Lawrence Journal-World in 2003.
He published two fitness books: 'Get Off Your Ass! The Definitive Guide to Losing Weight, Eating Healthy, and Living Longer' and 'Busting Your Butt and Gut: Minimizing Your Maximum Areas.'
He supports legalizing marijuana and using the tax revenue to fund public schools and health initiatives.
His campaign platform includes eliminating padded football for children under thirteen, daily physical education in public schools, and farm-to-table school meals to fight childhood obesity.
No public church affiliation, denomination, or faith background was identified in any campaign materials, news coverage, or public records reviewed.
whereTheyWorship: No public church affiliation or denomination was identified for Marty Tuley. His campaign website, social media accounts, news coverage, and public records do not reference any congregation, faith tradition, or religious involvement. In a state where most gubernatorial candidates make at least some reference to faith, this absence is notable.

quotes.text: I'm a fourth-generation Kansan with deep roots in our state's soil and values.
quotes.source: Tuley for Governor campaign website, About page

quotes.text: We have the geography, the work ethic and the heart to lead the country again in education, agriculture, energy and small business.
We want a candidate that's in the middle. Where's the middle? right here, I'm the middle. I think that I'm the best chance the democratic party has to win the general.
I'm not here to play party politics. I'm here to lead - with common sense, straight talk, and Kansas values.
quotes.source: Tuley for Governor campaign website, About page

quotes.text: My health and fitness platform prioritizes prevention, wellness, and access. I will implement daily physical education in public schools, eliminate padded football for children under 13, and promote farm-to-table meals to fight obesity. Legalizing marijuana will fund health initiatives and education. I support personal freedom in medical decisions and aim to reduce healthcare costs through better lifestyle support.
quotes.source: Tuley for Governor campaign website, Health and Fitness page

campaignFinance.narrative: Tuley's 2025 year-end filing with the Kansas Governmental Ethics Commission shows $2,600 raised, $193.89 spent, and $2,406.11 cash on hand. By gubernatorial standards, this is an extremely small campaign. For comparison, Democratic frontrunner Ethan Corson reported over $900,000 raised in the same period, and even underdog Cindy Holscher reported roughly $397,000. Tuley's filing lists only a handful of named contributors, consistent with a campaign that is largely self-contained with minimal outside financial support.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: No undisclosed or dark money contributions identified. The campaign's total fundraising is too small to attract PAC or outside spending.
